Goal id: G12 (goals ledger, milestone M48).

This is a FRESH goal in `clarifying` status with NO questions filed yet. Run your single-planner mode, perform exactly ONE state-driven step: read the goal, study the repository as needed, and file the FIRST batch of clarifying questions on the questions ledger (linked to goals:G12, milestone M48), then return the status token `awaiting-answers`.

The goal (verbatim): "Add live monitoring capabilities to both edge and concentrator instances. We already have some sort of a metrics API; embed a simple webpage which would receive dynamic status updates over WebSocket and display live link quality/peer/loss/RTT/FEC statistics. The /resilient-ws-ui skill (guidelines for reliable WebSocket connections to a backend with clear connection-health surfacing) could help with the frontend. Important open question: how to make such an API safe against unauthorized local calls."

Context you should verify against the actual repo before asking questions: the project is wanbond (Go, WireGuard bonding tunnel; edge + concentrator roles), there is an existing metrics facility (look at internal/ for a metrics/telemetry package and any HTTP listener), config lives in internal/config, docs in docs/design.md. Provenance on every ledger write: author="opus-4.8[1m]", session="be1a85fd-55c8-4654-ae42-672792fc0238".

End your reply with a `### Session summary` section per your contract.

```go
1	package sched

3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
5	)
6	
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency
90	// contract PathHealth documents), which is how one *Prober per path feeds liveness
91	// AND weight to the scheduler. The weighted scheduler's unit tests drive a synthetic
92	// PathQuality so the RTT/loss → weight formula is exercised on hand-built Estimates

94	type PathQuality interface {
95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}
97	
98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
```

```go
21	func main() {
22		if err := run(os.Args[1:]); err != nil {
23			fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "wanbond:", err)
24			os.Exit(1)
25		}
26	}
27	
28	// run is the testable entry point: it parses the command line, loads the
29	// configuration, brings the tunnel up for the configured role, and blocks until
30	// a termination signal, then tears the tunnel down.
31	func run(args []string) error {
32		if len(args) == 1 && (args[0] == "version" || args[0] == "--version") {
33			fmt.Println("wanbond", version)
34			return nil
35		}
36	
37		fs := flag.NewFlagSet("wanbond", flag.ContinueOnError)
38		configPath := fs.String("config", "", "path to the TOML configuration file (mode 0600)")
39		if err := fs.Parse(args); err != nil {
40			return err
41		}
42		if *configPath == "" {
43			return fmt.Errorf("--config is required (or `wanbond version`)")
44		}
45	
46		cfg, err := config.Load(*configPath)
47		if err != nil {
48			return err
49		}
50	
51		lg, err := log.New(cfg.Log.Level, os.Stderr)
52		if err != nil {
53			return err
54		}
55		main := lg.Component("main")
56		main.Info("wanbond starting", "version", version, "role", string(cfg.Role))
57	
58		tun, err := device.Up(cfg, lg)
59		if err != nil {
60			return err
61		}
62		defer tun.Close()
63	
64		main.Info("tunnel interface up", "interface", tun.Name(), "role", string(cfg.Role))
65	
66		// SIGINT/SIGTERM terminate; SIGHUP reloads the config file to add/remove paths
67		// on the running tunnel (T30). SIGHUP is idiomatic for a daemon config reload and
68		// needs no extra socket or privilege.
69		sig := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
70		signal.Notify(sig, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM, syscall.SIGHUP)
71		stopped := deviceStopped(tun)
72		for {
73			select {
74			case s := <-sig:
75				if s == syscall.SIGHUP {
76					reloadTunnel(main, tun, *configPath)
77					continue
78				}
79				main.Info("shutting down", "signal", s.String())
80				return nil
81			case <-stopped:
82				// Unrecoverable engine teardown: fail loud so the exit status reflects it
83				// and a supervisor (systemd Restart=on-failure) restarts the daemon.
84				return fmt.Errorf("tunnel device stopped unexpectedly")
85			}
86		}
87	}
88	
89	// reloadTunnel re-reads and validates the config file and applies its path diff to
90	// the running tunnel. A bad reload (unreadable, insecure-mode, invalid, or a failed
91	// path change) is LOGGED and the running tunnel is left intact — a reload must never
92	// tear down a live session. config.Load performs the fail-fast validation.
93	func reloadTunnel(lg log.Logger, tun *device.Tunnel, configPath string) {
94		cfg, err := config.Load(configPath)
95		if err != nil {
96			lg.Error("config reload rejected; keeping running config", "error", err.Error())
97			return
98		}
99		if err := tun.Reload(cfg); err != nil {
100			lg.Error("config reload failed to apply; tunnel still running", "error", err.Error())
101			return
102		}
103		lg.Info("config reloaded")
104	}
105	
106	// deviceStopped returns a channel that closes when the tunnel device tears down
107	// on its own (an unrecoverable engine error), so run can exit rather than block
108	// on a dead device.
109	func deviceStopped(t *device.Tunnel) <-chan struct{} {
110		done := make(chan struct{})
111		go func() {
112			t.Wait()
113			close(done)
114		}()
115		return done
116	}
```

```go
128			}
129			return nil
130		}
131	
132		// Hostname: resolve and require every resolved address to be loopback.
133		ips, err := net.LookupIP(host)
134		if err != nil {
135			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: cannot resolve listen host %q: %w", host, err)
136		}
137		if len(ips) == 0 {
138			return fmt.Errorf("metrics: listen host %q resolved to no addresses", host)
139		}
140		for _, ip := range ips {
141			if !ip.IsLoopback() {
142				return fmt.Errorf("%w: host %q resolves to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, host, ip)
143			}
144		}
145		return nil
146	}
147	
148	// verifyLoopbackBind is the fail-closed, act-then-verify half of the loopback
149	// guard: it asserts that the address the kernel ACTUALLY bound (ln.Addr()) is a
150	// loopback TCP address, independent of any DNS resolution. Because net.Listen
151	// resolves a hostname listen address itself — a second, independent lookup from
152	// requireLoopback's — verifying the concrete bound address is the only check
153	// that a resolver cannot subvert. A non-TCP or non-loopback bound address
154	// yields ErrNonLoopbackBind so the caller can close the listener and refuse.
155	func verifyLoopbackBind(a net.Addr) error {
156		tcp, ok := a.(*net.TCPAddr)
157		if !ok {
158			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-TCP address %v", ErrNonLoopbackBind, a)
159		}
160		if !tcp.IP.IsLoopback() {
161			return fmt.Errorf("%w: bound to non-loopback %s", ErrNonLoopbackBind, tcp.IP)
162		}
163		return nil
164	}
```

**`cmd/wanbond/main.go`** — main(function), run(function), reloadTunnel(function), deviceStopped(function)

```go
1	// Command wanbond is the wanbond tunnel daemon. A single binary serves both
2	// roles (edge and concentrator); the role is selected from configuration at
3	// runtime, not by which binary is invoked.
4	package main
5	
6	import (
7		"flag"
8		"fmt"
9		"os"
10		"os/signal"
11		"syscall"
12	
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/device"
15		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
16	)
17	
18	// version is overridden at build time via -ldflags "-X main.version=...".
19	var version = "dev"
20	
21	func main() {
22		if err := run(os.Args[1:]); err != nil {
23			fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "wanbond:", err)
24			os.Exit(1)
25		}
26	}
27	
28	// run is the testable entry point: it parses the command line, loads the
29	// configuration, brings the tunnel up for the configured role, and blocks until
30	// a termination signal, then tears the tunnel down.
31	func run(args []string) error {
32		if len(args) == 1 && (args[0] == "version" || args[0] == "--version") {
33			fmt.Println("wanbond", version)
34			return nil
35		}
36	
37		fs := flag.NewFlagSet("wanbond", flag.ContinueOnError)
38		configPath := fs.String("config", "", "path to the TOML configuration file (mode 0600)")
39		if err := fs.Parse(args); err != nil {
40			return err
41		}
42		if *configPath == "" {
43			return fmt.Errorf("--config is required (or `wanbond version`)")
44		}
45	
46		cfg, err := config.Load(*configPath)
47		if err != nil {
48			return err
49		}
50	
51		lg, err := log.New(cfg.Log.Level, os.Stderr)
52		if err != nil {
53			return err
54		}
55		main := lg.Component("main")
56		main.Info("wanbond starting", "version", version, "role", string(cfg.Role))
57	
58		tun, err := device.Up(cfg, lg)
59		if err != nil {
60			return err
61		}
62		defer tun.Close()
63	
64		main.Info("tunnel interface up", "interface", tun.Name(), "role", string(cfg.Role))
65	
66		// SIGINT/SIGTERM terminate; SIGHUP reloads the config file to add/remove paths
67		// on the running tunnel (T30). SIGHUP is idiomatic for a daemon config reload and
68		// needs no extra socket or privilege.
69		sig := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
70		signal.Notify(sig, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM, syscall.SIGHUP)
71		stopped := deviceStopped(tun)
72		for {
73			select {
74			case s := <-sig:
75				if s == syscall.SIGHUP {
76					reloadTunnel(main, tun, *configPath)
77					continue
78				}
79				main.Info("shutting down", "signal", s.String())
80				return nil
81			case <-stopped:
82				// Unrecoverable engine teardown: fail loud so the exit status reflects it
83				// and a supervisor (systemd Restart=on-failure) restarts the daemon.
84				return fmt.Errorf("tunnel device stopped unexpectedly")
85			}
86		}
87	}
88	
89	// reloadTunnel re-reads and validates the config file and applies its path diff to
90	// the running tunnel. A bad reload (unreadable, insecure-mode, invalid, or a failed
91	// path change) is LOGGED and the running tunnel is left intact — a reload must never
92	// tear down a live session. config.Load performs the fail-fast validation.
93	func reloadTunnel(lg log.Logger, tun *device.Tunnel, configPath string) {
94		cfg, err := config.Load(configPath)
95		if err != nil {
96			lg.Error("config reload rejected; keeping running config", "error", err.Error())
97			return
98		}
99		if err := tun.Reload(cfg); err != nil {
100			lg.Error("config reload failed to apply; tunnel still running", "error", err.Error())
101			return
102		}
103		lg.Info("config reloaded")
104	}
105	
106	// deviceStopped returns a channel that closes when the tunnel device tears down
107	// on its own (an unrecoverable engine error), so run can exit rather than block
108	// on a dead device.
109	func deviceStopped(t *device.Tunnel) <-chan struct{} {
110		done := make(chan struct{})
111		go func() {
112			t.Wait()
113			close(done)
114		}()
115		return done
116	}
```

fields.question: Network exposure: must the monitoring UI stay loopback-only (like the existing /metrics endpoint), or should it be reachable off-host (e.g. from a phone/laptop on the LAN behind the edge)?
fields.context: The existing metrics server (internal/metrics/server.go) enforces loopback-only binding as a hard fail-fast invariant (ErrNonLoopbackBind, plus an act-then-verify check on the kernel-bound address) because it exposes per-path operational data. A live-monitoring webpage is most useful viewed from a browser, which for loopback-only means either a browser on the box itself or an SSH port-forward. Allowing a non-loopback bind materially changes the security design (auth becomes mandatory, TLS becomes a question). This decision drives the whole plan shape.
suggestions: Loopback-only, same invariant as /metrics; remote viewing via `ssh -L` port-forward
Loopback default, but permit an explicit opt-in non-loopback bind gated on auth being configured (fail-fast if non-loopback without auth)
LAN-reachable by default with mandatory auth
fields.recommendation: Option 2: keep loopback as the default and the documented posture, but allow an explicit non-loopback bind that hard-requires the auth mechanism (fail-fast at config load otherwise). This keeps the safe default while making the UI actually usable from the LAN behind an edge box.

fields.question: Listener topology: should the UI + WebSocket share the existing metrics listener/mux (new routes next to /metrics on the same port), or get a dedicated listener with its own [monitor] config block and port?
fields.context: internal/metrics.Server currently serves exactly one route (/metrics, Prometheus text format) on the [metrics] listen address. Adding / (page) and /ws (live updates) to that mux couples the scrape surface to the UI surface: auth added for the UI would either also gate Prometheus scrapes (breaking scrapers) or need per-route exemptions. A separate listener keeps /metrics a bare unauthenticated Prometheus surface and lets the monitor listener carry auth uniformly, at the cost of a second port and config block.
suggestions: Same listener: add / and /ws to the metrics mux, auth-exempt /metrics
Same listener and auth everything including /metrics
fields.recommendation: Separate [monitor] listener. Uniform auth on the new surface, zero behaviour change to the existing scrape endpoint, and the exposure decision (previous question) can then differ between the two surfaces.

fields.question: Auth threat model and mechanism: which adversary must the local API resist, and which authentication mechanism do you want? (a) other unprivileged local users/processes on the same host calling the loopback API, (b) the operator's own browser being lured to a malicious page that attacks the localhost API cross-origin (DNS rebinding / CSRF), or (c) both?
fields.context: This is the goal's stated open question. The mechanisms differ per adversary. Against (b), strict Host/Origin validation on every request and on the WebSocket upgrade suffices and needs no secret. Against (a), a secret is required: a static bearer token in the TOML config (already enforced mode 0600, so readable only by the daemon user/root), or a per-boot random token written to a 0600 file and logged, either presented once via URL and then held in a cookie/localStorage. A Unix-domain socket with SO_PEERCRED gives the strongest local-caller identity but browsers cannot speak to unix sockets, so it fits a CLI, not an embedded webpage. Note the daemon runs privileged (TUN device), so on a single-admin box adversary (a) may be out of scope by definition.
suggestions: Origin/Host validation only (defends the browser vector; any local process can still read stats)
Static token in the 0600 TOML config + Origin/Host validation
Per-boot random token (0600 file, path logged at startup) + Origin/Host validation
Unix socket + SO_PEERCRED (CLI-only, incompatible with a browser UI)
fields.recommendation: Option 2: static token in the config (generated by the operator or documented `openssl rand` one-liner), presented as ?token= once and set as a SameSite=Strict cookie, plus unconditional Host/Origin validation on the WS upgrade and API routes. Covers both adversaries with no new files or state; the 0600 config already protects the secret exactly as it protects the WG private key.

fields.question: Is the monitoring UI strictly read-only (a dashboard), or should v1 also expose control actions (e.g. trigger failover, SIGHUP-equivalent config reload, enable/disable a path)?
fields.context: Read-only vs control changes the stakes of the auth question, the API surface, and the test burden substantially. The daemon already has SIGHUP for config reload, so a reload button would duplicate an existing control path. Everything the goal text names (link quality/peer/loss/RTT/FEC statistics) is read-only and already available via the metrics Source interface (Paths/FEC/Reseq/Session/PeerNames snapshots).
fields.recommendation: Read-only for v1. It matches the goal text, keeps the unauthorized-local-call risk to information disclosure rather than tunnel manipulation, and control actions can be a follow-up goal.

fields.question: Frontend toolchain constraint: hand-written vanilla HTML/CSS/JS embedded via go:embed (no Node/npm build step in the repo), or is introducing a JS build toolchain (e.g. Vite + a framework) acceptable? Relatedly: OK to add one Go WebSocket dependency (github.com/coder/websocket), or hand-roll the server side?
fields.context: The repo is currently pure Go (go.mod has no web/JS anything; golang.org/x/net is present but its websocket package is deprecated and unsuitable). A single embedded vanilla page keeps the build `go build`-only and the binary self-contained, at the cost of hand-writing the reconnect/health logic per the /resilient-ws-ui skill guidelines (which are framework-agnostic and fit vanilla JS fine). A toolchain buys niceties (TypeScript, components) but adds a second build system to CI and the release process. On the server side, coder/websocket is the maintained, minimal, widely-used choice; hand-rolling RFC 6455 is possible but not a good use of effort.
suggestions: Vanilla single-page, go:embed, zero JS toolchain; add coder/websocket for the server
Vanilla page but hand-rolled WS server (zero new deps)
fields.recommendation: Option 1: vanilla embedded page plus coder/websocket. Keeps the toolchain Go-only and the binary self-contained; one small, well-maintained dependency is the right trade against hand-rolling the WS protocol.

fields.question: Data scope and cadence for v1: which statistics, at what push interval, and with how much history? Specifically: (a) is the existing metrics Source snapshot set (per-(peer,path) RTT/jitter/loss/throughput/up, FEC counters, resequencer counters, WG session state) the right v1 payload, or is anything missing? (b) what update interval (e.g. 1s)? (c) instantaneous values only, or client-side rolling history with sparklines/graphs (last N minutes, browser memory only)? (d) on the concentrator, per-peer sections for all bound peers?
fields.context: The metrics Source interface (internal/metrics/metrics.go) already snapshots exactly the categories the goal names, so the cheapest design pushes those same snapshots as JSON over the WebSocket on a timer — no new instrumentation. Anything beyond it (e.g. per-path event log, handshake history, packet captures) means new plumbing in internal/telemetry/device and grows the plan. History kept server-side (ring buffers) vs client-side (browser only, lost on reload) is a real scope fork.
suggestions: Source snapshots as-is, 1s push, client-side-only rolling ~5min sparklines, per-peer sections on the concentrator
Source snapshots, 1s push, instantaneous values only (no graphs)
Snapshots + server-side ring-buffer history so a freshly opened page shows the recent past
fields.recommendation: Option 1. It reuses the existing snapshot plumbing verbatim, gives the 'live link quality' feel via client-side sparklines, and loses nothing that matters on page reload; server-side history can be a follow-up if wanted.

content.text:
Six clarifying questions filed (Q45–Q50), all linked to `goals:G12` under M48. The goal remains in `clarifying` — the server's open-question gate now blocks any phase transition until the user answers.

### Session summary
- **Did:** Rule 2(a) for fresh goal G12 (`clarifying`, no questions, not defect-seeded): grounded in the repo (codegraph over `internal/metrics`, `internal/config`, `cmd/wanbond/main.go`, plus go.mod), then filed the first batch of clarifying questions.
- **Achieved:** Created Q45 (network exposure: loopback-only vs LAN, against the existing `ErrNonLoopbackBind` invariant), Q46 (shared metrics listener vs dedicated `[monitor]` listener), Q47 (auth threat model + mechanism — the goal's stated open question, with token/Origin-validation/unix-socket options), Q48 (read-only vs control actions), Q49 (vanilla go:embed vs JS toolchain; coder/websocket dependency), Q50 (data scope/cadence/history, per-peer on concentrator). Each carries context, suggestions, and a recommendation. Goal G12 unchanged in `clarifying`.
- **Discovered:** The data plane for this goal already exists: `internal/metrics.Source` (Paths/FEC/Reseq/Session/PeerNames) snapshots exactly the statistics the goal names, and `internal/metrics/server.go` enforces loopback-only binding fail-fast with an act-then-verify check on the kernel-bound address. Repo is pure Go — no websocket library (x/net's is deprecated) and no JS toolchain; single binary serves both roles from TOML config (0600-enforced, SIGHUP reload).
- **Issues:** none.
